---
title: "Lab 2 — USV PID Tuning"
subtitle: "Week 7 · {{< var quarter >}}"
---

<!-- Internal: data collection runs through Week 7; analysis and
     performance characterization happen in Week 8. -->

## Objectives

Test and evaluate the closed-loop performance of the USV's speed and yaw-rate controllers in the following scenarios:

| Experiment | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Base** | This is our baseline setup, which has the ArduRover default PID gain values, no rate limiters and no feedforward gain. This is the reference against which the other two experiments are compared.  Goal is to inspect the default closed-loop behavior, both the  |
| **FeedForward** | Add only feedforward gain, keeping the rest of the PID to both speed and yaw-rate control loops.  Gain values from our open-loop system identification lab. | Stock PID gains |
| **Tuned** | Tune PID gains for "best" response | Feedforward kept on |

For each experiment, both the **speed (surge / throttle)** loop and the **yaw-rate (steering / rudder)** loop are exercised with manual step inputs and the responses logged.

For each of the six cases (three experiments × two channels), we will 
* Plot the closed-loop step response timeseries to visualize the transient and steady-state behavior
* Plot the PID internals (target vs actual, per-term breakdown) to understand how the controller is operating and generating the actuator commands.  This includes the \emph{controller effort} (P/I/D/FF) breakdown, which is critical for understanding the relative contribution of each term to the overall response and diagnosing issues like excessive overshoot (too much P), long settling time (too little D), or integrator windup (excessive I).  Controller effort is also a key aspect of the "best" response characterization, since we want to achieve good performance with minimal control effort.
* Quantify the transient performance metrics (rise time, settling time, overshoot) and the steady-state error to compare across the three experiments. 



## Deliverables

**This week:** clean step-response logs for all **three experiments × two channels = six datasets**, with a brief quality-check.

**Next week:** process the data with the quantify rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error, and compare across the three experiments.

## Required reading

Before the field session, read the **[Autopilot → Stabilization Layer](../../autopilot/stabilization.qmd)** reference. It documents the two control loops you'll be tuning — the speed loop and the yaw-rate loop — and is what makes the three experiments and the tuning workflow comprehensible.

What's in there: % CLAUDE: Remove the summary here.  This is tough to maintain as we are still actively developing the reference. 

- **Block diagram** of the speed and yaw-rate PID loops, including how the RC stick maps to a setpoint, the input/error filters, the PID block, and the actuator output.
- **`ATC_*` parameter tables** for both loops — full descriptions of the tunable PID gains, feedforward, filter cutoffs, and rate limiters. The Lab 2 experiments enable / disable subsets of these parameters.
- **DataFlash log-channel reference** — `PIDA_*` (speed) and `PIDS_*` (yaw-rate) PID internals, plus the `THR` / `STER` / `RCOU` / `GPS` channels used in the analysis figures.

The lab-specific framing — which `ATC_*` parameters change between Base / FeedForward / Tuned, and which stay fixed — is summarized in §3 of the [Procedure](procedure.qmd).

## Sub-pages

This page is the assignment overview. The lab-specific detail lives in:

- **[Procedure](procedure.qmd)** — pre-lab checklist, field workflow, step-input technique, and the at-the-field quality-check criteria.
- **[Example Results](results.qmd)** — example timeseries and PID-internals figures for each of the six cases (plus an integrator-windup demonstration), and a summary metrics table generated from the analysis pipeline.

## Materials

### Students

- ArduPilot Rover doc references — [`ardupilot_references.md`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/refs/ardupilot_references.md)
- Shared data repository: [ME2801_USV_Shared](https://nps01-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/bbingham_nps_edu/IgBrVQgpC7VsQ4pnhatXVfWBAatl-so1kOaAatxqty9Bj7g?e=C0EGzJ).  This is where to upload your raw (*.BIN) autopilot logs for processing. Add a subfolder for your team (e.g. `TeamX`) and upload there.  The instructors will convert the logs to MATLAB (*.mat) format,
- Analysis scripts — `book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/`
  - % CLAUDE: Add link to /home/bsb/WorkingCopies/me2801/introduction-to-feedback-control/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/closedloop_assess_example.m
  - % CLAUDE: Insert note that they will need the entire driectory for the helper functions, e.g. `plot_speed_response.m` and `plot_yaw_response.m` that generate the PID internals figures.

### Instructors
- [`closedloop_assess_main.m`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/closedloop_assess_main.m) — main driver: loads each log, plots, runs metrics, writes CSV + Markdown summary
- Log conversion — [`bin2mat.py`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/utils/ardu_utils/bin2mat.py)
